label: About
title: About Canned Publishing
disclosure: Translations and plain-language versions are AI-generated and may contain errors — read the original at the source for decisive details.
body:
Canned Publishing tries to put the puzzle together: to make Swedish politics, society and history understandable — and to separate what actually affects you from noise and propaganda. Everything is available in 13 languages, and each item is presented in several layers so you can choose how deep you want to go.

How we work

We gather material directly from primary sources — the Swedish Parliament, municipalities, regions, government agencies, news media, the EU and cultural-heritage databases — and run it through an AI pipeline that runs locally, without sending anything to the cloud. For each item we create three layers:

  • A short summary — what it is about, in ten seconds.
  • A plain-language explanation — the same thing in simple, stripped-down language.
  • The original text — the full source, always linked.

Everything is then translated into 13 languages. The translations and plain-language summaries are produced by AI and may contain errors or shifts in nuance — use them as a way in. For legally or factually decisive details, the original text always applies, and we always link to it. The source is always credited.

What we cover

We sort by who decides, because that determines what you can influence and where to turn:

  • The municipality — the closest level: schools, preschools, elderly care, social services, building permits, water and waste. (298 decisions tracked.)
  • The region — mainly health care and public transport. (36 matters.)
  • The state — laws and national policy via Parliament. (426 bills and parliamentary matters.)
  • Government agencies — those that implement and oversee: the Tax Agency, Social Insurance Agency, Migration Agency, Public Health Agency, the central bank, the National Audit Office and others. (54 announcements.)

Beyond that we cover news (10), world affairs and the EU (39), markets (the central bank's rate, the OMXS30 index) and Swedish history (198 articles). 1062 articles in Swedish so far.

Swedish history — and how sure we are

History is a puzzle of fragments, not a finished truth. That is why we mark every historical article with an evidence status instead of pretending everything is equally certain:

  • Type of source — does the claim rest on contemporary sources, later records, archaeology, interpretation or legend?
  • Certainty — from established through likely and contested to speculative and legend.
  • Flags — marking missing pieces, contested claims, logical oddities or a long gap in time between event and source.

We work through history era by era, from the Stone Age to the 20th century, with the notable people and events enumerated via Wikidata. The Viking Age also gets real runic inscriptions — the era's only domestic contemporary sources — straight from Uppsala University's runic database (Rundata), with transliteration and normalisation shown in the original.

News and the search for the truth

To get closer to what actually happened, we fetch the same news story from 59 media outlets in 11 countries and compare them. We translate all headlines into a Swedish hub, cluster those about the same event, and show where they agree and where they diverge.

Crucially, we weigh independence: ten copies of the same news-agency wire are one voice, not ten. Real consensus requires several different owners in several countries. State media are included — but hard-labelled (⚑) and never counted as independent confirmation; they are there precisely to show the propaganda angle, not to confirm facts.

The sources we weigh together

Sweden

  • Aftonbladet — independent social-democratic (Schibsted)
  • Dagens Industri — business-friendly (Bonnier)
  • Dagens Nyheter — independent liberal (Bonnier)
  • Expressen — independent liberal (Bonnier)
  • SVT Nyheter — centre / neutral (Public service)
  • Svenska Dagbladet — non-aligned moderate (Schibsted)
  • Sveriges Radio Ekot — centre / neutral (Public service)

Rest of the Nordics

Norway

  • Aftenposten — conservative (Schibsted)
  • Dagbladet — liberal-left (Aller)
  • NRK — centre / neutral (Public service)
  • VG — independent (Schibsted)

Denmark

  • Berlingske — conservative (Berlingske Media)
  • Børsen — business-friendly (JP/Politikens Hus)
  • DR — centre / neutral (Public service)
  • Jyllands-Posten — conservative-liberal (JP/Politikens Hus)
  • Politiken — centre-left (social-liberal) (JP/Politikens Hus)

Finland

  • Demokraatti — centre-left (Socialdemokraterna (SDP))
  • Helsingin Sanomat — liberal centre (Sanoma)
  • Hufvudstadsbladet — liberal (KSF Media)
  • Ilta-Sanomat — popular / sensational (Sanoma)
  • Iltalehti — popular / sensational (Alma Media)
  • Kansan Uutiset — left (Vänsterförbundet)
  • Kauppalehti — business-friendly (Alma Media)
  • Maaseudun Tulevaisuus — centre / rural (MTK)
  • Suomen Uutiset — right-wing populism (Sannfinländarna)
  • Verkkouutiset — centre-right (Samlingspartiet (Kokoomus))
  • Yle — centre / neutral (Yleisradio (public service))
  • Yle (svenska) — centre / neutral (Yleisradio (public service))

Germany

  • Bild — right-wing populist tabloid (Axel Springer)
  • Der Spiegel — centre-left (Spiegel-Verlag)
  • Deutsche Welle — centre / neutral (Tyska förbundsstaten) ⚑ state-funded
  • Die Welt — conservative (Axel Springer)
  • Die Zeit — liberal centre (Zeit-Verlag)
  • FAZ — conservative-liberal (FAZIT-Stiftung)
  • Handelsblatt — business-friendly (Handelsblatt Media)
  • Süddeutsche Zeitung — centre-left liberal (SWMH)
  • Tagesschau (ARD) — centre / neutral (ARD (public service))
  • taz — left (taz (kooperativ))

USA

  • AP — news agency (fact base) (Associated Press)
  • CNN — centre-left (Warner Bros. Discovery)
  • Fox News — right (Fox Corporation)
  • NPR — centre / centre-left (NPR (delvis offentligt finansierad))
  • New York Times — centre-left (NYT Company)
  • Wall Street Journal — centre-right / business (News Corp)
  • Washington Post — centre-left (Nash Holdings (Bezos))

Global lenses

India

  • Indian Express — liberal (Indian Express Group)
  • NDTV — pro-government (Adani-owned) (Adani Group)
  • The Hindu — centre-left (The Hindu Group)
  • Times of India — mainstream / establishment (Bennett Coleman)

Brazil

  • Estadão — centre-right liberal (Grupo Estado)
  • Folha de S.Paulo — centre (Grupo Folha)
  • G1 (Globo) — centre (Grupo Globo)

Qatar

  • Al Jazeera — state-funded (Global South angle) (Qatars stat) ⚑ state-funded

China

  • CGTN — state party line (Kinas stat) ⚑ state-controlled
  • China Daily — state party line (Kinas stat) ⚑ state-controlled
  • Global Times — state (nationalist) (Kinas kommunistparti) ⚑ state-controlled
  • Xinhua — state party line (Kinas stat) ⚑ state-controlled

Russia

  • RT — state (disinformation risk) (Rysslands stat) ⚑ state-controlled
  • TASS — state party line (Rysslands stat) ⚑ state-controlled

Languages

All content is available in 13 languages: Svenska, English, Deutsch, Français, Nederlands, Italiano, Español, Polski, Magyar, Română, Suomi, العربية, فارسی. Arabic and Persian are shown right-to-left.

Transparency

The pipeline runs locally on our own hardware — no texts are sent to external cloud-AI services. Every article states its source and links to the original, which is always what counts. AI is used for translation and plain language, never to invent content.
